intitule: Children's Rights and Duties: Word Recognition

enonce.texte: Read the list of words. Match each word to its correct definition.

questions.texte: Match the words on the left to the definitions on the right.
questions.reponse: 1. Education - c. The right to go to school and learn; 2. Protection - e. The right to be safe from harm; 3. Respect - a. To think of someone as important and treat them well; 4. Duty - b. Something you have to do; 5. Rights - d. Things you are allowed to have or do.

sousQuestions.texte: What is the difference between a right and a duty?
sousQuestions.reponse: A right is something you are allowed to have or do. A duty is something you have to do or are responsible for.

enonce.texte: Look at the pictures. Which of these is a child's duty? Write 'duty' next to the sentences that describe a duty.
images: A drawing of a child helping to wash dishes at home.
A drawing of a child attending school.
A drawing of a child playing a video game.
A drawing of a child respecting an elder.

questions.texte: Look at the drawings. Which actions show a child's duty? Write (D) for duty.
questions.reponse: 1. Helping to wash dishes - D; 2. Attending school - D; 3. Playing a video game - Not a duty; 4. Respecting an elder - D.

sousQuestions.texte: Why is it a child's duty to help at home?
sousQuestions.reponse: Because it helps the family and teaches responsibility.

enonce.texte: Read each statement carefully. Decide if it is a right (R) or a duty (D).

questions.texte: Identify if the following is a right or a duty. Justify your choice.
questions.reponse: 1. R - Every child has the right to an education; 2. D - Children must go to school; 3. R - Children have the right to food and shelter; 4. D - It is a child's duty to help their parents; 5. R - Children have the right to be protected from harm.

sousQuestions.texte: Why is 'going to school' both a right and a duty?
sousQuestions.reponse: It is a right because every child has the right to an education. It is also a duty because laws require children to attend school to learn.

intitule: Writing Sentences About Rights and Duties

enonce.texte: Complete the sentences below with the correct information about a child's rights and duties.

questions.texte: Fill in the blanks to make correct sentences.
questions.reponse: 1. Every child has the right to an education; 2. Children must respect their parents; 3. It is a child's duty to help at home; 4. Every child has the right to good health; 5. Children must obey their teachers.

sousQuestions.texte: Write 3 more sentences about children's rights.
sousQuestions.reponse: Example: Children have the right to play. Children have the right to be protected from abuse. Children have the right to a name and nationality.

enonce.texte:
Read the scenario and answer the questions below.

Mina is 11 years old. She goes to school every day and loves to learn. At home, she helps her mother clean the kitchen. She always respects her grandparents. One day, her classmate, Sido, tells her he cannot come to school because he has to work in the fields to help his family.

questions.texte: Based on the scenario, answer the following questions.
questions.reponse: 1. Mina has the right to education; 2. Some duties of a child are going to school, helping at home, and respecting elders; 3. Sido's right to education is being violated because he is working instead of going to school.

sousQuestions.texte: What is the right of a child that we see Mina exercising?
What duties of a child does Mina perform?
sousQuestions.reponse: Going to school, helping at home, and respecting her grandparents.

sousQuestions.texte: What right of Sido is being violated?

enonce.texte:
Read the scenario and answer the questions.

In your town, a new law is proposed to make it mandatory for all children to attend school until they are 16 years old. Some parents think this is a good idea, but others think children should be able to work to help their families.

questions.texte: Discuss the pros and cons of the proposed law in relation to children's rights and duties.
questions.reponse: Aspect 1: It ensures children have the right to education. Aspect 2: It may conflict with the duty to help family, especially if they rely on the child's income. Aspect 3: It upholds the duty of the state to protect children's rights.

sousQuestions.texte: Do you think every child should go to school until they are 16? Explain your answer.
sousQuestions.reponse: Example: Yes, because education is important for the future of the child and the country. It is a child's right to learn and develop their potential.

sousQuestions.texte: Why is it sometimes difficult for families to respect children's rights?
sousQuestions.reponse: Because some families are very poor and need children to work to survive.

sousQuestions.texte: What duty does the government have to protect children's rights?
sousQuestions.reponse: The government must make sure all children can go to school, protect them from harm, and provide for their basic needs like food and healthcare.

intitule: Creating a Poster about Children's Rights

enonce.texte: Create a simple poster for your classroom. The poster should list three rights of a child and one duty.

questions.texte: Design a poster about children's rights and duties.
questions.reponse: Example poster content: Title: 'MY RIGHTS AND DUTIES' with drawings of a child going to school, a child eating healthy food, and a child being protected. Under each image, write: 'Right to education', 'Right to health', 'Right to protection'. Below, write in a box: 'MY DUTIES: Go to school, Help at home, Respect my parents and teachers.'

sousQuestions.texte: What are the three rights you chose for your poster?
sousQuestions.reponse: Example: The right to education, the right to health, and the right to protection.

sousQuestions.texte: What duty of a child is important to remember?
sousQuestions.reponse: Example: It is the duty of every child to go to school and respect their teachers.
